patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious, tangential project manager. Open with: “I just can’t sleep anymore, and it’s getting worse.” Volunteer early: you lie awake for hours, wake repeatedly, can’t get back to sleep, months-long worsening, daytime fog affecting work, and family history (dad and grandparent). Ramble a bit about work stress and tracking sleep.

If asked, disclose: jumpy/creepy legs at night needing movement, bedtime anxiety, early awakenings, occasional snoring (unsure about apneas), unintentional weight loss, more night sweats, irritability, and long-standing inherited diabetes with more unpredictable sugars.

Only reveal later if rapport builds: brief confusion/word-finding issues when sleep-deprived, worse memory, nighttime racing heart/shaky episodes, increasing evening alcohol use, and overusing OTC sleep aids.

Resist admitting at first: sometimes using a family member’s sleep pills and cannabis gummies; if pressed, admit. Mention passive thoughts (“I can’t keep doing this”) but deny plan/intent.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe my cortisol is high,” “maybe nighttime sugar crashes,” and smartwatch shows “no deep sleep so something’s wrong with my brain.” Push for a prescription; worry about cost/coverage of sleep study and CBT-I. If clinician shows empathy, soften and share more details.

tool_call_sequence.description: Implements evidence-based treatment for insomnia with appropriate emphasis on CBT-I and safe, time-limited medication use when needed.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic based on duration/frequency.

Explains treatment and explicitly mentions CBT-I as first-line therapy, including at least one CBT-I component (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene as adjunct).
Assesses key insomnia features before treating: sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening, duration, frequency, daytime impairment, and precipitating/maintaining factors.

Screens for secondary causes and red flags: depression/anxiety, suicidality, substance use (alcohol/cannabis/stimulants), medication contributors (caffeine, decongestants, steroids), pain, GERD, thyroid symptoms, and shift work/circadian issues.

Screens for sleep disorders that change management: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome (urge to move legs, evening worsening), parasomnias; avoids sedative-hypnotics if high suspicion of untreated OSA.

Before prescribing, performs all required safety checks: asks about medication allergies (allergy_check), checks for drug-drug interactions with current meds/substances (drug_interaction_check), and checks contraindications (contraindication_check) relevant to the chosen agent and patient factors (pregnancy, severe hepatic impairment, glaucoma/urinary retention for anticholinergic agents, respiratory depression risk, older adult fall risk).
Uses shared decision-making and sets expectations: CBT-I timeline, medication is optional/short-term, goals are improved function and sleep efficiency rather than perfect sleep.
Provides safety counseling for hypnotics: next-day impairment, complex sleep behaviors (if Z-drug), fall risk, avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives, and secure storage.
Avoids inappropriate prescribing: no chronic benzodiazepines for insomnia; avoids multiple sedatives; avoids opioids for sleep; avoids initiating sedatives without assessing substance use risk.
Creates or verbalizes a follow-up interval (typically 2–4 weeks) to reassess symptoms, adherence to CBT-I, side effects, and need for deprescribing if medication was started.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, recommend CBT-I as first-line, and if symptoms are moderate/severe or urgent functional impairment, add a short-term hypnotic after required safety checks.

steps: Elicit insomnia pattern, duration, frequency, and daytime consequences; confirm adequate sleep opportunity.

Provide diagnosis explanation using the term insomnia; discuss acute vs chronic framework.
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I as first-line; outline core components and how to access (in-person, digital CBT-I).
If medication is indicated, complete allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check before selecting an agent; choose lowest-risk, lowest effective dose, short duration.
Counsel on medication risks and safe use; set plan to taper/stop once improved.
Arrange follow-up to reassess response and adverse effects and reinforce CBT-I.

acceptable_variants.description: CBT-I only with no medication when insomnia is mild, acute, or patient prefers nonpharmacologic therapy; still documents diagnosis and provides a clear plan for CBT-I access and follow-up.
steps: Assess insomnia characteristics, contributors, and screen for OSA/RLS and psychiatric comorbidity.
Explain insomnia diagnosis and why CBT-I is first-line.
Provide CBT-I plan (stimulus control + sleep restriction) and adjunct sleep hygiene guidance.
Set follow-up plan and criteria for considering short-term medication later (persistent impairment despite CBT-I, short-term crisis use).

acceptable_variants.description: Medication choice varies (e.g., low-dose doxepin for sleep maintenance; non-benzodiazepine hypnotic for sleep onset) while maintaining required safety checks and short-term use principles.
steps: Confirm insomnia subtype and comorbidities; screen for OSA/RLS and substance use.

patient.instructions:
